questions.question: Which one of the following is NOT equivalent to p <-> q? A. (not p or q) and (p or not q) B. (not p or q) and (q -> p) C. (not p and q) or (p and not q) D. (not p and not q) or (p and q)
questions.answer: C. (not p and q) or (p and not q)
questions.to_find: The option that is NOT logically equivalent to the biconditional p <-> q

understand.plain: The biconditional p <-> q is TRUE exactly when p and q have the same truth value (both true or both false) and FALSE when they differ. We test each option against this pattern. Three of the options reproduce 'p equals q' in different disguises, but one option actually describes the OPPOSITE case 'p differs from q' (the exclusive-or), so it is the odd one out. That mismatch is what makes option C not equivalent.

keywords.explain: True when p and q agree (TT or FF), false when they disagree; it can be written as (p -> q) and (q -> p).
keywords.example: p=T, q=T gives p <-> q = T; p=T, q=F gives p <-> q = F.

keywords.explain: p XOR q is TRUE exactly when p and q differ; it equals (not p and q) or (p and not q), the negation of the biconditional.
keywords.example: p=T, q=F gives XOR = T, which is the opposite of p <-> q = F.

keywords.explain: a -> b is the same as (not a) or b, which lets you rewrite the arrow options as pure or/and expressions for comparison.
keywords.example: q -> p becomes (not q) or p, i.e. p or not q.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='498' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>p <-> q is TRUE when p = q</text><text x='20' y='80' class='lbl ok'>A, B, D : describe p = q</text><text x='30' y='105' class='sub mut'>(equivalent to the biconditional)</text><text x='20' y='150' class='lbl no'>C : (not p and q) or (p and not q)</text><text x='30' y='175' class='sub mut'>= p XOR q  =  p differs from q</text><text x='20' y='215' class='big no'>C is NOT equivalent</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Panel showing options A, B, D all encode 'p equals q' while option C encodes 'p differs from q' (XOR), so C is the non-equivalent one.

given.aim: Identify which of the four expressions fails to be logically equivalent to p <-> q.

terms.meaning: First propositional variable
terms.example: p = T on a true row
terms.connects: Left side of the biconditional p <-> q

terms.meaning: Second propositional variable
terms.example: q = F on some row
terms.connects: Right side of the biconditional p <-> q

terms.meaning: Biconditional: true iff p and q are equal
terms.example: TT and FF rows are true, TF and FT rows are false
terms.connects: The reference expression each option is compared against

terms.term: Option C = (not p and q) or (p and not q)
terms.meaning: Exclusive-or of p and q, true iff they differ
terms.example: True on the TF and FT rows
terms.connects: Exact negation of the biconditional, hence not equivalent

given.plan: Rewrite each option in or/and form and compare its truth pattern to 'p equals q'; the one matching 'p differs from q' (XOR) is the non-equivalent answer.

steps.title: Pin down the target pattern
steps.formula_raw: (p <-> q) is true iff p = q ; equals (p -> q) and (q -> p)
steps.apply: p <-> q is true on rows TT and FF and false on TF and FT. Equivalently (p -> q) and (q -> p) = (not p or q) and (not q or p) = (not p or q) and (p or not q).
steps.note: This last and-of-disjunctions form is exactly option A, so A is equivalent.

steps.title: Rewrite B and D and confirm they match
steps.apply: B: (not p or q) and (q -> p) = (not p or q) and (not q or p) = the same and-form as A, so B is equivalent. D: (not p and not q) or (p and q) lists exactly the FF and TT rows, which is again 'p equals q', so D is equivalent.
steps.note: Both B and D reproduce the TT/FF true-rows of the biconditional.

steps.title: Expose C as exclusive-or
steps.formula_raw: p XOR q == (not p and q) or (p and not q) == not (p <-> q)
steps.apply: C = (not p and q) or (p and not q) is true on rows FT and TF, i.e. exactly when p and q DIFFER. That is the exclusive-or, the negation of the biconditional. Hence C is NOT equivalent to p <-> q.
steps.note: C true rows are precisely the false rows of p <-> q, the opposite pattern.

solution.result: Options A, B, D all equal 'p equals q' (the biconditional), while C equals 'p differs from q' (XOR), so C is NOT equivalent.

questions.formula_note: The biconditional means 'p equals q'; option C is exactly the exclusive-or 'p differs from q' = not(p <-> q), so C is the non-equivalent choice.

questions.question: Let p, q, r, s represent the following propositions. p : x in {8, 9, 10, 11, 12} ; q : x is a composite number ; r : x is a perfect square ; s : x is a prime number. The integer x >= 2 which satisfies not((p => q) and (not r or not s)) is ____________.
questions.to_find: The integer x >= 2 that makes the formula not((p => q) and (not r or not s)) true

understand.plain: We want the single integer x >= 2 for which the whole formula is TRUE. A negation is true exactly when the thing inside is FALSE, so we need (p => q) and (not r or not s) to be FALSE. A conjunction is false when at least one part is false. Working out which x in the relevant range breaks one of those parts, while respecting the propositions p, q, r, s, leads to exactly one answer, x = 11.

keywords.explain: not(A and B) is true exactly when (A and B) is false, i.e. when A is false OR B is false (De Morgan).
keywords.example: not((p => q) and (not r or not s)) is true iff (p => q) is false or (not r or not s) is false.

keywords.explain: p => q is false only on the single row p = true, q = false; in every other case it is true.
keywords.example: If x = 11 then p is true but q (composite) is false, so p => q is false.

keywords.explain: For a chosen x, each of p, q, r, s becomes a definite true/false fact about that number.
keywords.example: For x = 11: p true (11 in the set), q false (11 is prime, not composite), r false (not a square), s true (prime).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>x = 11</text><text x='20' y='75' class='sub ink'>p = T (in set)   q = F (prime, not composite)</text><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ink'>r = F (not square)   s = T (prime)</text><text x='20' y='150' class='lbl ink'>p => q = T => F = FALSE</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub mut'>so (p=>q) and (...) is FALSE, its negation is TRUE</text><text x='20' y='218' class='big ok'>answer x = 11</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Evaluation for x = 11: p true, q false makes p => q false, so the inner conjunction is false and its negation is true.

given.aim: Find the integer x >= 2 making not((p => q) and (not r or not s)) evaluate to true.

terms.meaning: True only for x equal to 8, 9, 10, 11 or 12
terms.example: p is true for x = 11, false for x = 7
terms.connects: Antecedent of the implication p => q

terms.meaning: True when x has a factor other than 1 and itself
terms.example: q true for 8, 9, 10, 12; false for the prime 11
terms.connects: Consequent of p => q; its failure makes p => q false

terms.term: r : x is a perfect square
terms.meaning: True when x = k^2 for some integer k
terms.example: r true for 9; false for 11
terms.connects: Appears as not r inside the disjunction (not r or not s)

terms.meaning: True when x has exactly two divisors
terms.example: s true for 11; false for 9
terms.connects: Appears as not s inside the disjunction (not r or not s)

given.plan: The whole formula is true iff the inner conjunction is false, so make either (p => q) false or (not r or not s) false; test the candidate integers in the set against the four propositions to land on the unique x.

steps.title: Negate the conjunction
steps.formula_raw: not(A and B) == (not A) or (not B)
steps.apply: The formula not((p => q) and (not r or not s)) is true exactly when (p => q) and (not r or not s) is false, i.e. when (p => q) is false OR when (not r or not s) is false (so r and s are both true).
steps.note: An integer cannot be both a prime and a perfect square, so 'r and s both true' is impossible; the only route is to make p => q false.

steps.title: Make the implication false
steps.formula_raw: p => q is false only when p = true and q = false
steps.apply: We need p true (so x in {8,9,10,11,12}) and q false (x not composite). Among 8,9,10,11,12 only 11 is not composite (it is prime). So x = 11 makes p true and q false, hence p => q false.
steps.note: 8,9,10,12 are composite, so for them p => q is true and the formula fails; 11 is the lone exception.

steps.title: Confirm x = 11 satisfies the formula
steps.apply: For x = 11: p => q = T => F = false, so (p => q) and (not r or not s) is false, and its negation is true. No other integer x >= 2 works (numbers outside the set make p false, hence p => q true, and then the conjunction can only be broken via the impossible r-and-s case). Thus x = 11.
steps.note: Uniqueness comes from the set restriction plus 11 being the only prime in it.

questions.formula_note: De Morgan turns the outer negation into 'inner conjunction is false'; since r and s cannot both hold, the only escape is p => q false, which forces x = 11 (the lone non-composite in the set).

questions.question: Consider the following expressions: i. false ; ii. Q ; iii. true ; iv. P or Q ; v. not Q or P. The number of expressions given above that are logically implied by P and (P => Q) is ___________.
questions.to_find: How many of the five listed expressions are logically implied by P and (P => Q)

understand.plain: An expression E is 'logically implied by' a formula F when E is true in every situation where F is true. So first find when the premise P and (P => Q) is true: this needs P true, and then P => Q forces Q true. So the premise is true only in the single world P = true, Q = true. We then just check each of the five expressions in that one world and count how many come out true.

keywords.explain: F implies E means: in every row where F is true, E is also true. Only the true-rows of F matter.
keywords.example: If F is true only when P=T, Q=T, then E is implied iff E is true at P=T, Q=T.

keywords.explain: True only when P is true and Q is true; this is just the conclusion of modus ponens packaged as a single formula.
keywords.example: P=T forces P => Q to demand Q=T, so the only satisfying world is (T, T).

keywords.explain: Evaluate i to v at P=T, Q=T; an expression is implied exactly when it is true there.
keywords.example: 'false' is false everywhere, so it is never implied.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='238' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Premise true only at P=T, Q=T</text><text x='20' y='75' class='sub no'>i. false  => FALSE (not implied)</text><text x='20' y='105' class='sub ok'>ii. Q = T (implied)</text><text x='20' y='135' class='sub ok'>iii. true = T (implied)</text><text x='20' y='165' class='sub ok'>iv. P or Q = T (implied)</text><text x='20' y='195' class='sub ok'>v. not Q or P = T (implied)</text><text x='20' y='230' class='big ok'>count = 4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: At the only true-world P=T,Q=T, expressions ii, iii, iv, v are true while i (false) is not, giving a count of 4.

given.aim: Count how many of i-v are true in every world where P and (P => Q) is true.

terms.meaning: Propositional variable
terms.example: Forced to true by the premise
terms.connects: Both a conjunct of the premise and the antecedent of P => Q

terms.meaning: Propositional variable
terms.example: Forced to true once P is true (via P => Q)
terms.connects: Consequent that the premise pins to true; appears in expressions ii, iv, v

terms.meaning: Formula whose only satisfying world is P=T, Q=T
terms.example: Equivalent to asserting both P and Q
terms.connects: Defines the single row at which the five expressions are tested

terms.meaning: false, Q, true, P or Q, not Q or P
terms.example: Evaluated at P=T, Q=T
terms.connects: Each is implied iff it is true in that world

given.plan: Determine the unique world satisfying the premise (P=T, Q=T), evaluate each of the five expressions there, and count the true ones.

steps.title: Find when the premise holds
steps.formula_raw: P and (P => Q) is true iff P = true and Q = true
steps.apply: P must be true; with P true, P => Q forces Q true (this is just modus ponens detaching Q). So the premise P and (P => Q) is satisfiable only in the single world P = T, Q = T.
steps.note: Because there is exactly one true-row, entailment reduces to evaluation at that row.

steps.title: Evaluate each expression at P=T, Q=T
steps.formula_raw: F implies E iff E is true in every true-row of F
steps.apply: i. false = FALSE -> not implied. ii. Q = T -> implied. iii. true = T -> implied. iv. P or Q = T or T = T -> implied. v. not Q or P = F or T = T -> implied.
steps.note: Only 'false' fails, because a contradiction is true in no world at all.

steps.title: Count the implied expressions
steps.apply: Four expressions (ii, iii, iv, v) are true at the premise's only true-world, so four are logically implied. Expression i is the lone exception.
steps.note: Constant 'true' (iii) is trivially implied by anything; constant 'false' (i) is implied by nothing satisfiable.

solution.result: Exactly 4 expressions (ii, iii, iv, v) are logically implied by P and (P => Q).

questions.formula_note: The premise is satisfiable only at P=T, Q=T; testing the five expressions there, all but the contradiction 'false' are true, giving the count 4.

questions.question: The statement (not p) => (not q) is logically equivalent to which of the statements below? I. p => q ; II. q => p ; III. (not q) or p ; IV. (not p) or q ; A. I only B. I and IV only C. II only D. II and III only
questions.to_find: Which of the four statements I-IV are logically equivalent to (not p) => (not q)

understand.plain: Rewrite the given conditional using 'a -> b equals not a or b'. So (not p) => (not q) becomes not(not p) or (not q) = p or not q. Now compare each candidate: its contrapositive q => p is always equivalent to it, and the disjunction (not q) or p is literally the same as p or not q. Statements I and IV instead simplify to not p or q, which is the opposite arrangement, so they are not equivalent.

keywords.explain: a -> b is logically the same as (not a) or b; use it to flatten every conditional into or-form for easy comparison.
keywords.example: (not p) => (not q) becomes p or not q.

keywords.explain: A -> B is equivalent to (not B) -> (not A); the contrapositive of (not p) => (not q) is q => p.
keywords.example: (not p) => (not q) and q => p are the same statement.

keywords.explain: p => q (statement I) is the inverse of the given conditional and is NOT equivalent; it reduces to not p or q.
keywords.example: p or not q (target) differs from not p or q (statement I).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>(not p) => (not q)  =  p or not q</text><text x='20' y='80' class='sub ok'>II. q => p = not q or p = p or not q  (match)</text><text x='20' y='110' class='sub ok'>III. (not q) or p = p or not q  (match)</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub no'>I. p => q = not p or q  (no)</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub no'>IV. (not p) or q = not p or q  (no)</text><text x='20' y='218' class='big ok'>II and III only (D)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Target reduces to p or not q; statements II and III match it while I and IV give the opposite not p or q, so the answer is II and III.

given.aim: Decide which of I-IV are logically equivalent to (not p) => (not q).

terms.meaning: First propositional variable
terms.example: Appears un-negated in the target or-form
terms.connects: Antecedent that is negated in the given conditional

terms.meaning: Second propositional variable
terms.example: Appears negated in the target or-form
terms.connects: Consequent that is negated in the given conditional

terms.meaning: Given conditional, equal to p or not q
terms.example: False only when p=F and q=T
terms.connects: Reference each candidate is matched against

terms.meaning: p => q, q => p, (not q) or p, (not p) or q
terms.example: Each flattened to or-form for comparison
terms.connects: II and III flatten to p or not q; I and IV flatten to not p or q

given.plan: Convert the target and every candidate into (not _ ) or _ form, then keep the candidates whose or-form is identical to the target p or not q.

steps.title: Flatten the target conditional
steps.apply: (not p) => (not q) = not(not p) or (not q) = p or not q. Its contrapositive (not(not q)) -> (not(not p)) = q => p, so statement II is automatically equivalent.
steps.note: Contraposition is an equivalence, so q => p (II) needs no further check.

steps.title: Match statement III and reject I, IV
steps.apply: III: (not q) or p = p or not q = the target, so III is equivalent. I: p => q = not p or q, and IV: (not p) or q = not p or q; both equal not p or q, which is the negated-p arrangement and differs from p or not q. So I and IV are NOT equivalent.
steps.note: Swapping which variable is negated changes the false-row, so I and IV fail.

solution.result: Only statements II (q => p) and III ((not q) or p) are equivalent to (not p) => (not q), giving option D.

questions.formula_note: Flattening to or-form, the target is p or not q; II is its contrapositive and III is the same disjunction, while I and IV give not p or q, so the answer is II and III only (D).

questions.question: Let p, q and r be propositions and the expression (p -> q) -> r be a contradiction. Then, the expression (r -> p) -> q is A. a tautology B. a contradiction C. always TRUE when p is FALSE D. always TRUE when q is TRUE
questions.answer: D. always TRUE when q is TRUE
questions.to_find: The nature of (r -> p) -> q given that (p -> q) -> r is a contradiction

understand.plain: Saying (p -> q) -> r is a contradiction means it is FALSE in every relevant world. An implication X -> r is false only when X is true and r is false, so this condition forces r to be FALSE (and p -> q to be true). Now plug r = false into the second expression: r -> p becomes false -> p which is vacuously TRUE, so (r -> p) -> q collapses to true -> q, which equals q. Therefore the second expression is just q, hence it is true exactly when q is true.

keywords.explain: If X -> r is always false, then in every world X is true and r is false; in particular r must be false.
keywords.example: (p -> q) -> r false everywhere => r = false and p -> q = true.

keywords.explain: An implication with a false antecedent is automatically true: false -> anything = true.
keywords.example: With r false, r -> p = false -> p = true.

keywords.explain: Once an antecedent is fixed true, A -> B simplifies: true -> q = q.
keywords.example: (r -> p) -> q becomes true -> q = q.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='238' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>(p -> q) -> r is a contradiction</text><text x='20' y='70' class='sub mut'>=> r = FALSE (and p -> q = TRUE)</text><text x='20' y='115' class='lbl ink'>(r -> p) -> q with r = FALSE</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub mut'>r -> p = FALSE -> p = TRUE (vacuous)</text><text x='20' y='180' class='lbl ink'>TRUE -> q = q</text><text x='20' y='220' class='big ok'>true exactly when q is TRUE (D)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The contradiction forces r false; then r -> p is vacuously true and (r -> p) -> q reduces to q, true iff q is true.

given.aim: Classify (r -> p) -> q given that (p -> q) -> r is a contradiction.

terms.meaning: Propositional variable
terms.example: Its value turns out not to affect the final reduced expression
terms.connects: Antecedent of p -> q and consequent of r -> p

terms.meaning: Propositional variable
terms.example: The reduced value of the target expression
terms.connects: Consequent of both expressions; the final result equals q

terms.meaning: Propositional variable forced to false by the given contradiction
terms.example: r = false in every world consistent with the hypothesis
terms.connects: Antecedent of r -> p; r = false makes that implication vacuously true

terms.meaning: Given to be a contradiction (false everywhere)
terms.example: Forces p -> q = true and r = false
terms.connects: Its contradiction status fixes r, which drives the reduction of the target

given.plan: Use the contradiction hypothesis to deduce r = false, substitute into (r -> p) -> q, simplify the vacuously-true antecedent, and read off the reduced expression.

steps.title: Extract r = false from the contradiction
steps.formula_raw: X -> r is false  iff  X = true and r = false
steps.apply: (p -> q) -> r being a contradiction means it is false in every world. An implication is false only when its antecedent is true and its consequent false, so for ALL worlds we must have (p -> q) = true and r = false. In particular r is false.
steps.note: A contradiction is false in every row, so the only-false condition must hold everywhere, fixing r = false.

steps.title: Substitute r = false into the target
steps.apply: With r = false, the inner implication r -> p = false -> p = true (vacuously). So (r -> p) -> q = true -> q.
steps.note: The value of p is irrelevant once r is false, since false -> p is true regardless.

steps.title: Reduce true -> q to q
steps.apply: true -> q simplifies to q. Hence (r -> p) -> q equals q under the hypothesis. So the expression is neither a tautology nor a contradiction; it is true exactly when q is true and false when q is false. That is option D.
steps.note: Because it equals q, options A (tautology), B (contradiction) and C (depends on p) are all ruled out.

solution.result: The expression (r -> p) -> q reduces to q, so it is always TRUE when q is TRUE (option D).

questions.formula_note: The contradiction forces r = false; then r -> p is vacuously true and (r -> p) -> q collapses to q, so it is true exactly when q is true (D).

formulas.name: Biconditional Truth Value
formulas.plain: (P <-> Q) is true iff P and Q have the same truth value; false iff they differ
formulas.whenToUse: Use to evaluate or simplify statements of the form 'P if and only if Q', or to compare an expression against the biconditional pattern.
formulas.trap: The biconditional is TRUE on the equal rows (TT, FF); its negation, the exclusive-or, is true on the differing rows (TF, FT). Do not swap them.
formulas.reference: Connectives and truth tables, propositional logic

formulas.name: Implication as Disjunction
formulas.plain: a -> b is logically equivalent to (not a) or b; it is false only when a is true and b is false
formulas.whenToUse: Use to flatten any conditional into or-form so two conditionals can be compared, or to read off exactly when an implication is false.
formulas.trap: The single false row is (a true, b false); also true -> b equals b and false -> b equals true. Mixing up which side is negated turns p or not q into not p or q.

formulas.name: Exclusive-Or Definition
formulas.plain: p XOR q is true exactly when p and q differ; it equals (not p and q) or (p and not q) and is the negation of the biconditional
formulas.whenToUse: Use to recognise the 'p differs from q' pattern and to see that it is the opposite of the biconditional.
formulas.trap: XOR is the NEGATION of the biconditional, not the same as it; an expression matching XOR is therefore NOT equivalent to p <-> q.
formulas.reference: Connectives and truth tables, propositional logic

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws
formulas.latex:
eg(A \wedge B) \equiv (
eg A \vee 
eg B), \quad 
eg(A \vee B) \equiv (
eg A \wedge 
eg B)
formulas.plain: not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B); not(A or B) = (not A) and (not B)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to push a negation inside a conjunction or disjunction, turning 'not(A and B)' into 'A is false or B is false'.
formulas.trap: Negation flips the connective: AND becomes OR and OR becomes AND. Forgetting to flip the connective is the classic mistake.

formulas.name: Modus Ponens
formulas.plain: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B
formulas.whenToUse: Use when you have a conditional and its antecedent is known true; detach the consequent. Also shows that A and (A -> B) is satisfiable only when both A and B are true.
formulas.trap: Requires the antecedent A itself to be asserted true; do not confuse with affirming the consequent (B and A -> B do NOT give A).

formulas.name: Logical Implication (Entailment)
formulas.latex: F \models E \iff (\forall \text{ rows: } F \text{ true} \Rightarrow E \text{ true})
formulas.plain: F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
formulas.whenToUse: Use to test whether one formula entails another: examine only the true-rows of the premise F and check E there.
formulas.trap: Only the rows where F is true matter; a contradiction (false everywhere) is implied by nothing satisfiable, while a tautology (true everywhere) is implied by everything.

formulas.name: Contrapositive Equivalence
formulas.plain: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to rewrite a conditional in an equivalent form, often to recognise an equivalent statement among options.
formulas.trap: The contrapositive is equivalent, but the converse (B -> A) and the inverse (not A -> not B) are NOT equivalent to the original.

formulas.name: Vacuous Truth of Implication
formulas.plain: an implication with a false antecedent is automatically true (false -> anything = true)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to simplify a conditional once you know its antecedent is false; the whole implication evaluates to true regardless of the consequent.

terms.means: the antecedent, known to be false
the consequent, whose value is now irrelevant

formulas.trap: Vacuous truth applies only when the antecedent is false; do not assume an implication is true just because the consequent is unknown.
formulas.reference: Truth tables for implication, propositional logic
